In my view, the report shows how the war is increasingly moving toward a “gray-zone” escalation dynamic. Belarus plays a particularly sensitive role because Moscow appears to be seeking ways to increase pressure on Ukraine without opening a full-scale northern ground front.

At the moment, a Belarusian ground invasion still appears highly unlikely. Such an operation would require major troop concentrations, logistical preparation and significant Russian reserve capacity that currently does not seem available. Using Belarusian territory for drone and missile operations, however, is a very different matter. That option carries lower political and military risks for Moscow while still increasing pressure on Ukrainian air defenses and rear areas.

It is also notable that some Russian officials appear to be softening earlier rhetoric about systematically striking Kyiv. That may indicate that Ukraine’s expanding deep-strike drone campaign is creating real pressure on Russian rear-area security and air defense systems. Restrictions in Moscow’s airspace and the temporary closure of Kaliningrad airport are symbolically and politically important developments.

Modern warfare increasingly shows that strategic depth no longer guarantees security. Ukraine’s drone campaign demonstrates how relatively low-cost systems can create serious disruption hundreds of kilometers from the frontline. Over time, this may force Russia to significantly adapt its internal security and air defense priorities.

One of the key questions in the coming months will be how far Belarus becomes an active operational platform for Russian strikes. The deeper Belarus becomes integrated into the conflict, the greater the potential regional and NATO security implications for Eastern Europe.

Today, May 27, on the same day EU foreign ministers are meeting in Limassol to discuss the possibility of direct dialogue with Russia, former German Chancellor Angela Merkel said in the WDR 0630 podcast that she hopes the war in Ukraine will end "in 10 years - and in such a way that Ukraine is an independent, free, and sovereign country."

This is Merkel’s third public appearance on the same platform within ten days, and it coincides with active discussions in Brussels over who could represent the EU in negotiations with Moscow.

Merkel’s candidacy as an unofficial diplomatic actor has a fundamental problem: she is simultaneously the most acceptable and the least legitimate option.

Her very appearance in the public space looks staged. She's made three public appearances on the same platform within ten days.  On May 18, at the WDR Europaforum, Merkel said that Europe is "not using its diplomatic potential sufficiently", then, later that day, she rejected the idea of taking on an official mediator role. Today, May 27, on the WDR 0630 podcast, Angela Merkel said she hopes the war will end "in 10 years." Between these two appearances, Politico published an EU shortlist: Angela Merkel, Alex Stubb, Mario Draghi, and Putin’s negotiator Kirill Dmitriev publicly reacted to Merkel's remarks. The Kremlin is listening to her. The coincidence with the Brussels discussions is too much to be an accident.

Merkel is acceptable for this role because she is not discredited like Schröder and not viewed as an enemy by Moscow like Kaja Kallas. But this is a passive quality: "not the worst option" is not the same as "the right choice."

The Minsk process is not merely a "ballast," as Brussels diplomats softly describe it. It is a failure that Russia took use of to prepare for a full-scale invasion. Merkel herself admitted in a 2022 interview that Minsk gave Ukraine "time to strengthen itself." But this is simultaneously an admission that the negotiations were conducted not for peace, but for delay. If this is her diplomatic methodology, then Merkel is not suited for a role that requires a real settlement.

Merkel is no longer in power - and she herself emphasized this as an argument against her official candidacy. But then another question arises: what is her mandate in the unofficial track? Who authorized her? To whom is she accountable? An unofficial channel without an institutional anchor is either a private initiative the Kremlin can ignore, or parallel diplomacy that undermines the EU’s official position.

And, finally, today’s statement about "10 years" undermines Merkel's own claim that Europe is failing to use its diplomatic potential. If a person calling for more active diplomacy is herself setting a decade-long horizon, then this is either an absence of strategy or preparation of public opinion for a frozen conflict. Neither option makes Merkel a strong diplomatic actor.

The Wagner Group rebellion was a brief uprising led by Yevgeny Prigozhin against Russia’s military leadership during the Ukraine war. 

Prigozhin accused officials like Sergei Shoigu of corruption and poor leadership. 

In June 2023, Wagner troops seized the military headquarters in Rostov-on-Don and marched toward Moscow.

Russian President Vladimir Putin called the rebellion “treason” and warned of civil war. 

However, the mutiny ended within 24 hours after a deal brokered by Alexander Lukashenko. 

Wagner forces withdrew, and Prigozhin later moved to Belarus. 

The rebellion failed, but it exposed weaknesses and tensions inside Russia’s government and military.

Prigozhin died in an airplane "crash" on 23 August 2023

If Wagner had succeeded, Russia could have faced major political chaos or even civil conflict.

The Russia-Ukraine war has really shown how smart weapons and new tactics can change everything on the battlefield. Ukraine might be outnumbered but they've used some really effective systems that gave them a serious edge. 

Here are the ones that stood out the most, along with what Pakistan has in comparison:

1.Javelin Anti-Tank Missiles

Those American fire-and-forget missiles that wrecked Russian tanks early on. Super easy to use, top-attack mode, and perfect for hit-and-run ambushes.

Pakistan's Option: Our Baktar-Shikan (HJ-8) and Kornet-E missiles. Locally made versions are solid, and they're mounted on HIT Maaz vehicles for quick mobile strikes.

2. HIMARS Rocket Systems

Precision strikes on ammo dumps, command centers, and bridges. The way they shoot and scoot kept the Russians guessing.

Pakistan's Option:
Fatah-1, Fatah-2, and Fatah-3 MLRS. Our own indigenous rockets with good range and accuracy – built for exactly this kind of fast, deep strikes.

3. Bayraktar TB2 Drones

Turkish drones that became legends for recon and precision hits, even taking out ships and tanks.

Pakistan's Option:
We already fly TB2s ourselves, plus Wing Loong drones from China and our local systems. They're fully integrated and proven in our forces.

4. FPV Drones & Loitering Munitions

Cheap kamikaze drones everywhere – spotting targets, taking out tanks, and creating chaos deep behind lines. Game-changers in this war.

Pakistan's Edge:
We've got a strong drone program running with Turkish and Chinese help, and we're developing our own too. Lots of focus on this area.

5. Patriot Systems & Western Artillery

Holding the skies against missiles and drones, plus advanced howitzers that hit back hard.

Pakistan's Alternatives: 
HQ-9 air defense systems and our reliable artillery like M109 self-propelled guns, SH-15 howitzers and rocket launchers.

Bottom line:
This war proved that mobility, precision, and drones matter way more than just having big numbers. Ukraine got creative with what they had, and it worked. Pakistan has built a balanced, modern arsenal with home-grown stuff like Al-Khalid tanks, JF-17 jets, and Fatah rockets. We're ready if needed.

Full Context Q&A: “Jared the Investor”
This phrase originates from a specific moment in a U.S. press conference in Israel and has been widely discussed in connection with Jared Kushner’s role in Gaza ceasefire and reconstruction efforts.
Q: What exactly happened? What was the full question and answer?
Reporter’s question (Holly Williams, CBS News, during the Q&A portion of the press conference):
“The ceasefire deal took months to negotiate. What do you think the likelihood is that it will last long-term, if you had to put a number on it, a percentage?”
JD Vance’s response (Vice President, October 21/22, 2025, at a Civilian Military Cooperation Center / press event in Israel, alongside Jared Kushner, Steve Witkoff, and Admiral Brad Cooper):
“Well, Jared’s the investor here. I’m not going to give you a percentage. But look, what we’ve seen the past week gives me great optimism the ceasefire is going to hold, and if we get from where we were a week ago to a long-term, durable peace between Israel and Gaza, there are going to be hills and valleys, there are going to be moments where it looks like things aren’t particularly well. But given that and given the history of conflict, I think that everybody should be proud of where we are today.”
Vance went on to praise the team (explicitly naming Kushner and Witkoff) and emphasized that the U.S. would not put “boots on the ground” in Gaza while supporting an international security force.

Q: Who is “Jared” and why did Vance call him “the investor”?
Jared Kushner is President Trump’s son-in-law, a real estate developer by background, and an informal advisor/envoy in the second Trump administration. He has no official government title or Senate-confirmed position but has been a central figure in the administration’s Middle East diplomacy and Gaza-related efforts, working closely with real estate investor and special envoy Steve Witkoff.
Vance’s phrasing appears to be a light or deflecting reference to:
•Kushner’s prominent behind-the-scenes role in the peace/reconstruction track.
•Kushner’s real estate expertise and business history.
•The fact that Kushner was standing right there with the team during the press conference.

•A coastal tourism zone along the seafront with capacity for up to 180 skyscrapers (many envisioned as hotels/resorts).

Entering Rafah ‘whether there’s a ceasefire or not’

One example cited by Miller came in April 2024 as Israel was threatening to invade the Gaza Strip’s southern city of Rafah, which Netanyahu at the time was characterizing as Hamas’s final stronghold in the enclave.

🔳 When war criminal Nickolay Mladenov and the so-called “Board of Peace” unveiled their roadmap for Gaza after the October 2025 ceasefire, the language was polished carefully for international audiences. Stabilisation. Reconstruction. Civilian governance. Humanitarian recovery. Transition. Security.

The Board of Peace (BoP) is a U.S.-led international body established under the Trump administration to oversee the post-ceasefire phase in Gaza, including reconstruction, stabilization, and governance transition.19
Origins and Establishment
•Announcement: Proposed in September 2025 as part of Trump’s 20-point “Comprehensive Plan to End the Gaza Conflict.”
•UN Endorsement: UN Security Council Resolution 2803 (November 17, 2025) welcomed it, authorizing support for Gaza reconstruction and a temporary International Stabilization Force (ISF). Russia and China abstained.20
•Formal Launch: Charter signed/ratified by Trump at the World Economic Forum in Davos on January 22, 2026. Inaugural meeting held February 19, 2026, in Washington, D.C.21
Core Mission (Gaza-Focused)
•Oversee implementation of the ceasefire and Trump’s 20-point plan.
•Supervise the National Committee for the Administration of Gaza (NCAG) — a Palestinian technocratic body (not Hamas) meant to handle day-to-day administration during a transition period.
•Facilitate reconstruction (estimated $50–70+ billion needed), humanitarian aid distribution, and disarmament of Hamas.
•Deploy an International Stabilization Force (ISF) for security and training a new Palestinian police force.30
•Goal: Transition toward more stable governance (potentially linking to the Palestinian Authority long-term), while preventing Hamas resurgence.
Structure and Leadership
•Chairman: Donald Trump (named “Chairman for life” in the charter, with sole authority to nominate a successor).
•Multi-tiered: Executive Board (small group with significant U.S. influence), broader membership of countries and leaders.
•Members include the U.S., Israel (as a founding member), and various Arab and other nations. Major Western allies (e.g., parts of EU) have been hesitant or declined, citing concerns over it bypassing traditional UN mechanisms.27
•Funded partly by pledges (U.S. committed $10 billion; others ~$7 billion announced early on), but reports as of May 2026 indicate significant shortfalls in actual disbursements.
Broader Scope and Criticisms
The charter expanded beyond Gaza to a global mandate for peacebuilding in conflict zones, leading critics to call it a potential “alternative to the UN” — more nimble but U.S.-centric and lacking standard oversight.28
Challenges and Current Status (as of May 2026):
•Hamas has not fully disarmed, stalling full implementation and reconstruction.
•Limited progress on the ground: Israeli security zones remain, violence persists (though major fighting stopped), and NCAG officials are reportedly not yet fully operational in Gaza.
•Funding and donor fatigue issues; some pledges unfulfilled.
•Political controversies: Accusations of insufficient Palestinian input, risks of favoring Israeli security priorities, and questions about long-term viability.35
In summary, the Board of Peace represents Trump’s attempt at a pragmatic, results-oriented alternative to traditional multilateral efforts for Gaza’s postwar phase. It has UN blessing but faces skepticism, implementation hurdles, and questions about whether it can deliver lasting stability amid deep-seated conflicts. The situation remains fluid and contested.

The top diplomat from the Board of Peace has blamed Hamas for the stalled ceasefire, but critics have said the US-backed board’s lack of even-handedness in implementing the truce risks a return to war.

The “high representative for Gaza”, Nickolay Mladenov, told the UN security council on Thursday that Hamas was the “principal obstacle” to the ceasefire’s continued implementation because “it refused to accept verified decommissioning, relinquish coercive control and allow a genuine civilian transition”. Hamas rejected the Bulgarian diplomat’s accusations. Its spokesman, Hazem Qassem, said the report “reflects continued adoption of the Israeli position and serves as an attempt to justify further Israeli escalation”.    Critics of the Board of Peace, launched by Donald Trump in January, said the Mladenov report gave a misleading and one-sided account of the ceasefire, in which Israel has been the main violator.

Israeli forces have continued to carry out airstrikes on Gaza. They have also moved forward from the ceasefire line agreed in October, increasing the area under direct Israeli control from the agreed 53% to at least 60%, and have regularly shot at Palestinians who came within a few hundred metres of the shifting line. More than 850 Palestinians have been killed since the ceasefire was declared in October. Israel has also fallen short of its obligation to allow in 600 trucks of humanitarian supplies a day, and has refused to relax restrictions on “dual-use” items, which have prevented aid agencies bringing in basic humanitarian supplies, such as water pipes, or heavy machinery to begin clearing rubble.  Israel was not directly criticised in Mladenov’s report, only by implication in references to “all parties”. Critics said that by putting the blame solely on Hamas, Mladenov’s report could lend legitimacy to Benjamin Netanyahu to return to war.

___________________________________________________

Dear Mr President,
Honorable Representatives,
Dear friends and allies, dear Americans,
It is on Memorial Day, May 25, that I chose to write this letter to you.

It has already become a tradition in Ukraine, and I believe it always will be for every President of Ukraine, to address the United States on this day with words of gratitude.

In Ukraine, as we defend our Independence, we hold men and women in uniform in the highest respect. Without their courage Ukraine simply would not exist today. That is why we feel especially grateful to America's men and women in uniform, and every American.

Time and again, America has helped protect Europe and defend freedom. And today, with your experience and steadfast support, you continue helping us defend ourselves against Russia's unprovoked and ruthless aggression.

We know that the world would be a much darker place without American leadership and determination. And we deeply value everything you do to protect freedom and save human lives. For that I thank you.
Just ahead of Memorial Day, on the night of May 24, Russia launched an especially cruel massive attack against Ukraine.

Russia used 54 cruise, 30 ballistic, 3 Tsirkon hypersonic missiles and 2 Kinzhal air-launched ballistic missiles. The Putin-Trump linkage...

On April 29, 2026, Putin phoned Trump and offered Russian assistance in securing Iranian nuclear material as part of a broader negotiation that included Ukraine. Trump rebuffed the offer. The substance of the call, reported by @Bloomberg from Kremlin sources, was unambiguous about the linkage. Russia views Ukraine and Iran as a SINGLE strategic file. The United States, under Trump, has pretended to treat them as separate files. The mismatch is the strategic problem.

For Russia, Ukraine is the western anchor of the same Eurasian competition that Iran anchors in the south. NATO weapons flowing into Ukraine and American carriers operating off Iran are, in the Russian strategic framing, two fronts of the same encirclement. The Trump administration has communicated that it will pressure Ukraine toward a settlement on terms favourable to Russia while prosecuting the war on Iran toward an outcome favourable to the United States. 

Russia has not accepted that framing. 

Russia is providing Iran with weapons, food, dual-use components, and diplomatic cover through the same Caspian corridor that the Israeli March strikes were attempting to interdict. The trade is now bilateral: Russia receives political tolerance for its Ukraine position in exchange for sustaining Iranian capacity to resist the American campaign.

Trump's August 2025 Azerbaijan-Armenia peace deal, which created the "Zangezur Corridor" with a 99-year US lease, was the early strategic move in this competition. The corridor bypasses Iran's northern frontier and gives the United States a transit option that does NOT touch Iranian territory. 

Putin understood the move precisely. 

Within months, Russia accelerated the Rasht-Astara financing with a $1.6 billion loan, doubled down on Caspian shipping, and signed the January 2025 Comprehensive Strategic Partnership with Iran. The two leaders are running parallel corridor wars in the same geographic space. Iran sits at the intersection. The damage done to Iran is the variable that determines which corridor architecture survives the next decade.

This is no longer a war about uranium enrichment caps. This is a war between two corridor architectures, one running through American-controlled maritime chokepoints, the other running through Russian and Chinese-controlled overland infrastructure, with Iran as the keystone. The damage done to Iran is damage to the alternative architecture. The damage to the alternative architecture is damage to Russian, Chinese, and Indian strategic interests that those actors will not absorb indefinitely. Bookmark this.

"Putin and Trump Have Run Out of Options": The FT Compares the War in Ukraine and the U.S. Operation Against Iran

The Financial Times has published a scathing article in which it calls the Kremlin’s actions in Ukraine and the U.S. military operation against Iran the major geopolitical mistakes of our time. In both cases, the dictators and leaders miscalculated, and China emerged as the main beneficiary.

Key points from the analysts:

A trap for dictators: Both Putin and Trump counted on weak opponents and a quick victory within a few days. Instead, both have become bogged down in wars that are draining their countries.

The Kremlin’s Humiliation: Russia is losing 35,000 troops a month, while the Ukrainian Armed Forces are striking infrastructure 1,000 km deep into Russian territory. It has come to the point where Putin, through Trump, asked Zelenskyy not to attack Red Square with drones during the May parade.

Zelenskyy has gained new leverage: While the Pentagon is depleting its missile stockpiles in the Persian Gulf, Ukraine has revolutionized its military economy by shooting down expensive missiles with inexpensive domestic interceptors. Now the U.S. agency itself needs these Ukrainian technologies, giving Kyiv significant leverage over Trump.

The collapse of the former world order: U.S. influence in the Middle East has been undermined. Due to its aggression, Russia now has a border with NATO that is twice as long (due to Finland’s accession). The world is entering an era where middle powers (such as Ukraine and Iran) are proving they can put the giants in their place.

🔴WAR ON TERRORISM: HAMAS🔴/Times of Israel

Israel targets new Hamas military chief Mohammed Odeh, 11 days after killing his predecessor

No immediate confirmation if strike successful as 3 reported killed in Gaza City; Netanyahu and Katz vow Israel to ‘continue pursuing everyone who took part in the Oct. 7 massacre’

By Nava Freiberg and Nurit Yohanan

Israel on Tuesday evening carried out a strike in the Gaza Strip targeting new Hamas military chief Mohammed Odeh, 11 days after killing his predecessor.

Gaza’s civil defense agency, which operates as a rescue service under Hamas, reported at least three people were killed and 20 wounded in the strike in the Rimal neighborhood of western Gaza City.

There was no immediate comment from Hamas or official confirmation from Israel that Odeh was dead. Hebrew media outlets cited initial assessments indicating the strike was successful, with an Israeli source telling the Kan public broadcaster that Odeh was killed.

A joint statement from Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Defense Minister Israel Katz said Odeh was head of Hamas intelligence during the October 7, 2023, attacks, and last week was appointed to succeed Izz al-Din al-Haddad as the terror group’s leader in the Gaza Strip and chief of its military wing.

Al-Haddad was killed in a similar strike in Gaza City on May 15.

“Odeh was responsible for the murder, abduction and injury of many Israeli civilians and IDF soldiers,” said Netanyahu and Katz.

According to the Saudi A-Sharq Al-Awsat outlet, Odeh was close to al-Haddad and worked with him to “renew the organizational structure” of Hamas following the assassination of its former leaders Muhammad Deif and Muhammad Sinwar during the war sparked by the October 7 invasion of southern Israel.

Citing a source familiar with the details, the report said Odeh was initially approached to lead the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades following Sinwar’s assassination in May 2025, but declined.

However, the two other sources said they could not confirm that it was the case. Sinwar’s brother Yahya was the head of Hamas before being killed by the IDF in May 2024.

The report added that Odeh was tasked with gathering intelligence on IDF bases near the Gaza border and on weak points in the military’s Gaza Division in the run-up to October 7.

Odeh, estimated to be in his late 40s to early 50s, grew up in Gaza and was reported to have been involved with Hamas his whole life. In the past, he was involved with the group’s security unit that sought out Israeli spies.

Israel previously targeted Odeh several times, including a strike on his father’s house in Gaza in 2025 killed his eldest son, Amr.

“We will continue to pursue everyone who took part in the October 7 massacre,” said Netanyahu and Katz. “Sooner or later, Israel will reach them all.”

Despite the ceasefire that has been in place in Gaza since October, Israel has kept up its campaign against the perpetrators of October 7, with a report last week in the Wall Street Journal saying it has created a list of all Palestinians who took part in the attack and is working to kill or arrest each one.

According to the report, the list includes all Gazans who were identified as having crossed the border on October 7, as well as all Hamas leaders involved in orchestrating the massacre, during which some 1,200 people were killed and 251 taken hostage, mostly civilians, in the deadliest massacre of Jews since the Holocaust.

Along with al-Haddad and Odeh, the IDF announced last week it killed a Hamas operative who invaded Israel on October 7, without naming him.
